source.definition: to draw in liquid, air, or another substance by creating a vacuum or using suction

explanation.sourceText: The English verb "suck" originates from Old English "sūcan," which refers to the action of drawing in liquid or air by creating a vacuum. It is related to similar Germanic words and reflects the physical act of suction.
explanation.targetText: Verbul englezesc „suck“ provine din engleza veche „sūcan“, care desemnează acțiunea de a trage lichide sau aer prin crearea unui vid. Este înrudit cu cuvinte germanice similare și reflectă actul fizic de suctiune.

source.additional: This verb commonly describes the action of drawing in air or liquids by suction. It is often used in contexts involving vacuum cleaners (e.g., "they suck the carpet") or animals feeding by sucking.

target.definition: a ridica sau a capta ceva cu ajutorul unui aparat de suctiune

explanation.sourceText: The Romanian verb "a sug" derives from the Latin "sugere," meaning "to suck." It historically describes the action of drawing in liquids or air by suction.
explanation.targetText: Verbul românesc „a sug“ derivă din latinul „sugere“, care înseamnă „a aspira“. Este folosit pentru a descrie acțiunea de a prelua lichide sau aer prin supt.

target.additional: Verbul este folosit frecvent în legătură cu animale (de exemplu, un vițel care suge la mamelon) și cu aspiratoarele. De asemenea, poate avea sens figurat, însemnând „a exploata“.

examples.sourceText: Does the vacuum cleaner suck well?
The plant sucks water from the soil.
Do you suck the pudding powder through the straw?
examples.targetText: Sugăți pulberea de budincă prin pai?

examples.sourceText: The babies suck on their bottles.
I have sucked up the water.
He sucked every morning with the vacuum cleaner.
examples.targetText: El sugea în fiecare dimineață cu aspiratorul.

examples.sourceText: I will suck up the water.
I would suck up the water if I had a pump.
examples.targetText: Aș sugera apa dacă aș avea o pompă.

learningTips.text: Remember "a sug" sounds like "suck" in English, which helps you recall the action of drawing in liquid or air.
Think of a baby sucking on a bottle to connect the Romanian "a sug" with the English "suck."
Imagine a vacuum cleaner that sucks up dirt—this links "a sug" with the idea of suction and cleaning.
Remember that "a sug" can also mean to exploit or take advantage, like someone 'sucking' all the benefits out of a situation.
